cluster.title: Top 50 Kicking Athletes Exercise Library
cluster.purpose: Build kicking athletes who can access usable hip range, organize the support leg, chamber and recoil with control, rotate powerfully, strike or kick with high intent, tolerate repeated kicking loads, and recover safely.
source_basis: exercise_card_details_for_llm.md was the available Card v2 source for phase-first authoring, canonical phase keys, taxonomy key+weight tags, publish-gate expectations, dosage, scaling, safety, regimen, phaseProfile, and the preferred camelCase authoring JSON shape.

Purpose.txt was requested and searched for, but no file literally titled Purpose.txt surfaced in accessible File Library results for this run. This caveat is preserved instead of pretending the file was read.

User focus: kicking athletes across kickboxing, MMA, and soccer. Exercises were selected for hip access, support-leg control, chamber/recoil mechanics, rotational power, striking/ball-contact output, single-leg strength, adductor/hamstring/calf tissue capacity, deceleration, repeatability, and recovery.
cluster.authoring_format: Card v2 Authoring format: camelCase fields, canonical primaryPhaseKey/subrole/slot, taxonomy key+weight tags, movementRequirements, coachingExecution, dosage, scaling cohorts, pairingLogic, safety, regimen, and phaseProfile.

categories.title: Prepare & Access: Hip, Ankle, Trunk, and Kicking-Chain Access
categories.focus: Low-fatigue mobility and activation that opens hip rotation, adductors, hip flexors, ankles, calves, and trunk positions needed before technical kicking, striking, and ball work.

categories.title: Movement Intelligence: Chamber, Pivot, Balance, and Kick Skill Mapping
categories.focus: Fresh nervous-system drills that teach support-foot pivoting, hip control, chamber/re-chamber, recoil, target touch accuracy, ball-feel, and rhythm without turning the block into conditioning.

categories.title: Output: Rotational Power, Elastic Projection, and Reactive Kicking Speed
categories.focus: High-intent, low-volume drills for hip snap, rotational power, approach striking, pad/ball reaction, re-acceleration, and elastic support-leg stiffness while the athlete is fresh.

categories.title: Capacity: Single-Leg Strength, Adductor Reserve, Hip Flexor Force, and Trunk Transfer
categories.focus: Progressive strength exercises that build the force and tissue reserve behind powerful kicks: support-leg strength, lateral strength, posterior chain, hip extension, hip flexion, anti-rotation, and sled projection.

categories.title: Resilience, Repeatability, and Restore: Brakes, Lower-Leg Durability, Kick Volume, and Downshift
categories.focus: Controlled deceleration, landing, adductor/hamstring/lower-leg resilience, contact tolerance, repeat kick conditioning, and a cooldown reset so kicking capacity does not collapse under fatigue.

cluster.programming_rule: Phase order matters: Prepare & Access -> Movement Intelligence -> Output -> Capacity -> Resilience -> Sustained Capacity -> Restore. Kicking speed and skill come before fatigue; conditioning comes late; restore work stays low intensity.

cards.name: 90/90 Hip Switch with Reach
cards.category: Prepare & Access: Hip, Ankle, Trunk, and Kicking-Chain Access
cards.cardSummary: 90/90 Hip Switch with Reach is a Prepare & Access exercise for kicking athletes that trains hip rotation access with direct carryover to roundhouse chamber, side-kick chamber, soccer striking hip rotation.
cards.bestPlacement: Use early after a brief temperature raise and before high-speed kicking, sparring, sprinting, strength, or ball-striking work.
cards.description: 90/90 Hip Switch with Reach uses hip internal rotation, hip external rotation, thoracic rotation to improve a key kicking-athlete problem: Builds controlled hip internal and external rotation so the athlete can chamber, pivot, and re-chamber without borrowing motion from the knee or low back.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: mobilize. Coach this as Prepare & Access work: preserve the intended phase, dose the drill by quality, and connect every rep to support-leg control, hip action, trunk transfer, and safe reset behavior.
cards.athleteLanguage: Rotate the hip, not the low back.
cards.selectionRationale: Builds controlled hip internal and external rotation so the athlete can chamber, pivot, and re-chamber without borrowing motion from the knee or low back.

movementRequirements.space_needed: Open lane, mat, wall, rack, or target area with enough room to pivot, kick, strike, land, and reset safely.

cards.whyItWorks: Builds controlled hip internal and external rotation so the athlete can chamber, pivot, and re-chamber without borrowing motion from the knee or low back. Kicking athletes need the swing leg, stance leg, trunk, and foot to solve a shared timing problem: create range or force, transfer it through the hip and trunk, contact the target or ball, and recover to stance. This drill isolates one link without losing the sport relationship.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Prepare & Access because it improves access, activation, or readiness with minimal fatigue before technical kicking, output, strength, or conditioning.
cards.commonMisuse: Do not turn this into a long mobility test, passive stretch contest, or fatigue circuit. The drill should leave the athlete more ready, not tired or guarded.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by reducing range, speed, load, reaction choices, partner pressure, impact, or total volume first. Progress only one variable at a time and only after the athlete owns the quality gate.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Perform 90/90 Hip Switch with Reach with attention to hip internal rotation, hip external rotation, thoracic rotation, then reset into a stance or position that would allow another kick, strike, or ball action.
setup: Set up with none, mat and enough open space for the athlete to move, pivot, and stop without crowding.
Explain the intended phase and quality gate before the first rep so the athlete knows whether this is access, skill, output, strength, resilience, conditioning, or restore work.
Establish stance foot pressure, rib-pelvis position, and a safe return/reset position before adding speed, load, target, or partner input.
Connect the drill to roundhouse chamber, side-kick chamber so the athlete understands the transfer target without rushing the movement.
execution_steps: Move into the start position and establish breathing, foot pressure, and joint alignment.
Perform the rep smoothly through the available range without forcing or rushing.
Return to the start position under control and check that the athlete feels more ready.
Repeat only while the motion gets cleaner rather than more strained.
coach_cues: Rotate the hip, not the low back.
common_faults: Chasing range or speed before control
Support foot collapsing or twisting
Leaning through the low back
Letting the kick or movement finish in a poor stance
breathing_cues: Exhale to organize the ribs before effort.
Do not hold breath unless specifically bracing for a loaded strength rep.
Recover nasal or calm breathing between quality attempts.
coachingExecution.quality_gate: A rep counts only if 90/90 Hip Switch with Reach keeps pain-free range, organized breathing, clean support-foot pressure, controlled hip/trunk position, and a safe reset that supports roundhouse chamber, side-kick chamber.

dosage.notes: Use the low end when the athlete is learning, returning from layoff, in-season, sore, or carrying a high kicking/striking/soccer load. — 5-8/side

scaling.youth_beginner: Use no load, smaller range, fewer choices, and a wall or coach support when needed. Keep 90/90 Hip Switch with Reach controlled and prioritize stance, hip, trunk, and foot position before speed or load. 1-2 light sets; stop before fatigue or frustration.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use the base version with low impact and clear success criteria; add only one variable at a time. 2 sets of clean reps or short attempts with full coaching reset.
scaling.teen: Progress range, speed, load, or partner/target complexity only after the athlete can own the support leg and recoil/finish. Use the default dose; cap volume during growth spurts or heavy sport weeks.
scaling.adult_beginner: Use regressions for balance, mobility, and impact. Prioritize pain-free positions and slower execution. Start at the low end of sets, reps, and RPE.
scaling.adult_advanced: Progress by adding intent, load, speed, smaller reaction windows, or sport-specific angles while preserving the quality gate. Use default-to-high end volume only when quality remains stable.
scaling.older_adult: Use supported versions, lower range, lower impact, and longer rests. Avoid sudden pivots or high-speed kicks unless already prepared. 1-2 sets; RPE should stay conversational except for medically cleared strength work.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use qualified guidance. Avoid breath-holding, pain, pelvic-floor symptoms, hard impact, collision, and aggressive end range; choose supported low-intensity alternatives. Short, symptom-free sets only.

avoid_after: fatiguing kick intervals before skill learning
max impact on sore groin or hamstrings
chaotic partner contact without clear roles
pairingLogic.coach_note: For kickboxing/MMA/soccer sessions, place mobility and skill mapping before output, strength after speed/skill, resilience after primary work, conditioning late, and restore last.

readiness_checks: Pain-free hip, knee, ankle, and low-back range for the chosen version
Athlete can balance or use support without compensating
Clear space, target, partner roles, and stop command when applicable
contraindications: Acute groin, hamstring, hip, knee, ankle, or low-back pain without clearance
Uncontrolled dizziness, concussion symptoms, or inability to balance safely
Contact, pad, or partner work without communication and appropriate protective equipment
common_risks: Groin or hip-flexor irritation from forcing height
Knee torque from poor support-foot pivot
Hamstring or adductor overload from too much speed or range
Fatigue-driven loss of recoil and stance

regimen.typical_frequency: 1-3x/week depending on sport practice, soreness, and phase of season; low-intensity access drills can be used more often.
regimen.in_season_note: Reduce volume and keep only the highest-transfer options when sport practices already include high kicking, sprinting, or ball-striking load.
regimen.off_season_note: Progress range, load, intent, and complexity gradually while separating high-output days from heavy tissue-capacity days when needed.

mediaInternalNotes: Film the support foot and pelvis as much as the kicking leg.
Quality beats height, load, and volume.
Do not import as API snake_case without taxonomy facetId mapping.

cards.description: Step-Behind Lateral Bound to Stick uses hip abduction, knee flexion, ankle stiffness to improve a key kicking-athlete problem: Trains the lateral hip power and landing control needed to close distance, angle off, or recover after a wide kick.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: deceleration_cod_power. Coach this as Output work: preserve the intended phase, dose the drill by quality, and connect every rep to support-leg control, hip action, trunk transfer, and safe reset behavior.
cards.athleteLanguage: Push wide, land quiet, freeze the edge.
cards.selectionRationale: Trains the lateral hip power and landing control needed to close distance, angle off, or recover after a wide kick.

movementRequirements.space_needed: Open lane, mat, wall, rack, or target area with enough room to pivot, kick, strike, land, and reset safely.

cards.whyItWorks: Trains the lateral hip power and landing control needed to close distance, angle off, or recover after a wide kick. Kicking athletes need the swing leg, stance leg, trunk, and foot to solve a shared timing problem: create range or force, transfer it through the hip and trunk, contact the target or ball, and recover to stance. This drill isolates one link without losing the sport relationship.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Output because the drill expresses speed, power, elasticity, reactive intent, or striking output and therefore needs freshness, low volume, and enough rest to stay sharp.
cards.commonMisuse: Do not turn output work into conditioning or pad-volume fatigue. If speed, accuracy, recoil, landing, or decision quality drops, the set is over.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by reducing range, speed, load, reaction choices, partner pressure, impact, or total volume first. Progress only one variable at a time and only after the athlete owns the quality gate.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Perform Step-Behind Lateral Bound to Stick with attention to hip abduction, knee flexion, ankle stiffness, then reset into a stance or position that would allow another kick, strike, or ball action.
setup: Set up with none, cones and enough open space for the athlete to move, pivot, and stop without crowding.
Explain the intended phase and quality gate before the first rep so the athlete knows whether this is access, skill, output, strength, resilience, conditioning, or restore work.
Establish stance foot pressure, rib-pelvis position, and a safe return/reset position before adding speed, load, target, or partner input.
Connect the drill to angle change after kick, soccer lateral plant so the athlete understands the transfer target without rushing the movement.
execution_steps: Set the start position and visualize the target movement before the rep begins.
Execute one crisp high-intent rep with full commitment to speed, projection, rotation, or strike quality.
Finish with a controlled recoil, landing, catch, or stance reset rather than drifting out of position.
Rest fully before repeating; each attempt should look fresh and intentional.
coach_cues: Push wide, land quiet, freeze the edge.
common_faults: Chasing range or speed before control
Support foot collapsing or twisting
Leaning through the low back
Letting the kick or movement finish in a poor stance
breathing_cues: Exhale to organize the ribs before effort.
Do not hold breath unless specifically bracing for a loaded strength rep.
Recover nasal or calm breathing between quality attempts.
coachingExecution.quality_gate: A rep counts only if Step-Behind Lateral Bound to Stick keeps pain-free range, organized breathing, clean support-foot pressure, controlled hip/trunk position, and a safe reset that supports angle change after kick, soccer lateral plant.

dosage.notes: Use the low end when the athlete is learning, returning from layoff, in-season, sore, or carrying a high kicking/striking/soccer load. — 2-4/side

scaling.youth_beginner: Use no load, smaller range, fewer choices, and a wall or coach support when needed. Keep Step-Behind Lateral Bound to Stick controlled and prioritize stance, hip, trunk, and foot position before speed or load. 1-2 light sets; stop before fatigue or frustration.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use the base version with low impact and clear success criteria; add only one variable at a time. 2 sets of clean reps or short attempts with full coaching reset.
scaling.teen: Progress range, speed, load, or partner/target complexity only after the athlete can own the support leg and recoil/finish. Use the default dose; cap volume during growth spurts or heavy sport weeks.
scaling.adult_beginner: Use regressions for balance, mobility, and impact. Prioritize pain-free positions and slower execution. Start at the low end of sets, reps, and RPE.
scaling.adult_advanced: Progress by adding intent, load, speed, smaller reaction windows, or sport-specific angles while preserving the quality gate. Use default-to-high end volume only when quality remains stable.
scaling.older_adult: Use supported versions, lower range, lower impact, and longer rests. Avoid sudden pivots or high-speed kicks unless already prepared. 1-2 sets; RPE should stay conversational except for medically cleared strength work.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use qualified guidance. Avoid breath-holding, pain, pelvic-floor symptoms, hard impact, collision, and aggressive end range; choose supported low-intensity alternatives. Short, symptom-free sets only.

avoid_after: fatiguing kick intervals before skill learning
max impact on sore groin or hamstrings
chaotic partner contact without clear roles
pairingLogic.coach_note: For kickboxing/MMA/soccer sessions, place mobility and skill mapping before output, strength after speed/skill, resilience after primary work, conditioning late, and restore last.

readiness_checks: Pain-free hip, knee, ankle, and low-back range for the chosen version
Athlete can balance or use support without compensating

contraindications: Acute groin, hamstring, hip, knee, ankle, or low-back pain without clearance
Uncontrolled dizziness, concussion symptoms, or inability to balance safely
Contact, pad, or partner work without communication and appropriate protective equipment
common_risks: Groin or hip-flexor irritation from forcing height
Knee torque from poor support-foot pivot

Fatigue-driven loss of recoil and stance

regimen.typical_frequency: 1-3x/week depending on sport practice, soreness, and phase of season; low-intensity access drills can be used more often.
regimen.in_season_note: Reduce volume and keep only the highest-transfer options when sport practices already include high kicking, sprinting, or ball-striking load.
regimen.off_season_note: Progress range, load, intent, and complexity gradually while separating high-output days from heavy tissue-capacity days when needed.

Quality beats height, load, and volume.

cards.cardSummary: Cossack Squat is a Capacity exercise for kicking athletes that trains frontal-plane strength and range with direct carryover to side kick strength, low kick defense, soccer lateral reach.
cards.bestPlacement: Use after skill and output priorities. Pair with enough rest that strength and position quality remain the target.
cards.description: Cossack Squat uses hip abduction, knee flexion, ankle dorsiflexion to improve a key kicking-athlete problem: Develops strength through lateral hip and adductor ranges that kickers need but often only stretch passively.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: frontal_plane_lower_body_strength. Coach this as Capacity work: preserve the intended phase, dose the drill by quality, and connect every rep to support-leg control, hip action, trunk transfer, and safe reset behavior.
cards.athleteLanguage: Push the floor and own the side-to-side range.
cards.selectionRationale: Develops strength through lateral hip and adductor ranges that kickers need but often only stretch passively.

movementRequirements.space_needed: Open lane, mat, wall, rack, or target area with enough room to pivot, kick, strike, land, and reset safely.

cards.whyItWorks: Develops strength through lateral hip and adductor ranges that kickers need but often only stretch passively. Kicking athletes need the swing leg, stance leg, trunk, and foot to solve a shared timing problem: create range or force, transfer it through the hip and trunk, contact the target or ball, and recover to stance. This drill isolates one link without losing the sport relationship.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Capacity because the exercise builds strength, force production, tissue tolerance, and structural reserve through progressive loading or leverage.
cards.commonMisuse: Do not chase load at the expense of joint position, support-leg alignment, or trunk control. Strength only transfers if the athlete owns the shape.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by reducing range, speed, load, reaction choices, partner pressure, impact, or total volume first. Progress only one variable at a time and only after the athlete owns the quality gate.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Perform Cossack Squat with attention to hip abduction, knee flexion, ankle dorsiflexion, then reset into a stance or position that would allow another kick, strike, or ball action.
setup: Set up with none, kettlebell and enough open space for the athlete to move, pivot, and stop without crowding.
Explain the intended phase and quality gate before the first rep so the athlete knows whether this is access, skill, output, strength, resilience, conditioning, or restore work.
Establish stance foot pressure, rib-pelvis position, and a safe return/reset position before adding speed, load, target, or partner input.
Connect the drill to side kick strength, low kick defense so the athlete understands the transfer target without rushing the movement.
execution_steps: Start each rep from a balanced setup with the trunk braced and the working foot connected to the floor.
Lower or load through the intended joint action without twisting the knee, arch, pelvis, or low back to steal range.
Drive through the working leg or trunk line with controlled intent while keeping the target tissues doing the work.
Reset breathing and alignment between reps; add load only when the same positions repeat.
coach_cues: Push the floor and own the side-to-side range.
common_faults: Chasing range or speed before control
Support foot collapsing or twisting
Leaning through the low back
Letting the kick or movement finish in a poor stance
breathing_cues: Exhale to organize the ribs before effort.
Do not hold breath unless specifically bracing for a loaded strength rep.
Recover nasal or calm breathing between quality attempts.
coachingExecution.quality_gate: A rep counts only if Cossack Squat keeps pain-free range, organized breathing, clean support-foot pressure, controlled hip/trunk position, and a safe reset that supports side kick strength, low kick defense.

dosage.notes: Use the low end when the athlete is learning, returning from layoff, in-season, sore, or carrying a high kicking/striking/soccer load. — 4-8/side

scaling.youth_beginner: Use no load, smaller range, fewer choices, and a wall or coach support when needed. Keep Cossack Squat controlled and prioritize stance, hip, trunk, and foot position before speed or load. 1-2 light sets; stop before fatigue or frustration.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use the base version with low impact and clear success criteria; add only one variable at a time. 2 sets of clean reps or short attempts with full coaching reset.
scaling.teen: Progress range, speed, load, or partner/target complexity only after the athlete can own the support leg and recoil/finish. Use the default dose; cap volume during growth spurts or heavy sport weeks.
scaling.adult_beginner: Use regressions for balance, mobility, and impact. Prioritize pain-free positions and slower execution. Start at the low end of sets, reps, and RPE.
scaling.adult_advanced: Progress by adding intent, load, speed, smaller reaction windows, or sport-specific angles while preserving the quality gate. Use default-to-high end volume only when quality remains stable.
scaling.older_adult: Use supported versions, lower range, lower impact, and longer rests. Avoid sudden pivots or high-speed kicks unless already prepared. 1-2 sets; RPE should stay conversational except for medically cleared strength work.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use qualified guidance. Avoid breath-holding, pain, pelvic-floor symptoms, hard impact, collision, and aggressive end range; choose supported low-intensity alternatives. Short, symptom-free sets only.

avoid_after: fatiguing kick intervals before skill learning
max impact on sore groin or hamstrings
chaotic partner contact without clear roles
pairingLogic.coach_note: For kickboxing/MMA/soccer sessions, place mobility and skill mapping before output, strength after speed/skill, resilience after primary work, conditioning late, and restore last.

readiness_checks: Pain-free hip, knee, ankle, and low-back range for the chosen version
Athlete can balance or use support without compensating
Clear space, target, partner roles, and stop command when applicable
contraindications: Acute groin, hamstring, hip, knee, ankle, or low-back pain without clearance
Uncontrolled dizziness, concussion symptoms, or inability to balance safely
Contact, pad, or partner work without communication and appropriate protective equipment
common_risks: Groin or hip-flexor irritation from forcing height
Knee torque from poor support-foot pivot
Hamstring or adductor overload from too much speed or range
Fatigue-driven loss of recoil and stance

regimen.typical_frequency: 1-3x/week depending on sport practice, soreness, and phase of season; low-intensity access drills can be used more often.
regimen.in_season_note: Reduce volume and keep only the highest-transfer options when sport practices already include high kicking, sprinting, or ball-striking load.
regimen.off_season_note: Progress range, load, intent, and complexity gradually while separating high-output days from heavy tissue-capacity days when needed.

mediaInternalNotes: Film the support foot and pelvis as much as the kicking leg.
Quality beats height, load, and volume.
Do not import as API snake_case without taxonomy facetId mapping.

cards.category: Resilience, Repeatability, and Restore: Brakes, Lower-Leg Durability, Kick Volume, and Downshift
cards.cardSummary: 90/90 Breathing with Hip Reset is a Restore exercise for kicking athletes that trains cooldown hip and nervous-system reset with direct carryover to post-kicking recovery, low-readiness reset, restore phase.
cards.bestPlacement: Use at the end of the session only, after kicking, conditioning, strength, or sparring work is complete.
cards.description: 90/90 Breathing with Hip Reset uses hip flexion support, pelvic repositioning, rib expansion to improve a key kicking-athlete problem: Downshifts the athlete after kicking volume and helps recover hip, pelvis, and rib position without adding more training stress.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: breathing_downshift. Coach this as Restore work: preserve the intended phase, dose the drill by quality, and connect every rep to support-leg control, hip action, trunk transfer, and safe reset behavior.
cards.athleteLanguage: Exhale long, let the ribs drop, and leave the hips calmer.
cards.selectionRationale: Downshifts the athlete after kicking volume and helps recover hip, pelvis, and rib position without adding more training stress.

movementRequirements.space_needed: Open lane, mat, wall, rack, or target area with enough room to pivot, kick, strike, land, and reset safely.

cards.whyItWorks: Downshifts the athlete after kicking volume and helps recover hip, pelvis, and rib position without adding more training stress. Kicking athletes need the swing leg, stance leg, trunk, and foot to solve a shared timing problem: create range or force, transfer it through the hip and trunk, contact the target or ball, and recover to stance. This drill isolates one link without losing the sport relationship.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Restore because the goal is to downshift the nervous system, restore breathing, reduce tension, recover range, and support readiness without adding new stress.
cards.commonMisuse: Do not make this aggressive stretching, extra conditioning, or skill practice. The athlete should leave calmer and less guarded.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by reducing range, speed, load, reaction choices, partner pressure, impact, or total volume first. Progress only one variable at a time and only after the athlete owns the quality gate.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Perform 90/90 Breathing with Hip Reset with attention to hip flexion support, pelvic repositioning, rib expansion, then reset into a stance or position that would allow another kick, strike, or ball action.
setup: Set up with mat, wall and enough open space for the athlete to move, pivot, and stop without crowding.
Explain the intended phase and quality gate before the first rep so the athlete knows whether this is access, skill, output, strength, resilience, conditioning, or restore work.
Establish stance foot pressure, rib-pelvis position, and a safe return/reset position before adding speed, load, target, or partner input.
Connect the drill to post-kicking recovery, low-readiness reset so the athlete understands the transfer target without rushing the movement.
execution_steps: Move into the supported position slowly and find a calm, pain-free range.
Use long exhales and relaxed inhales while keeping the hips, ribs, and jaw quiet.
Allow the target tissues to reduce tone without forcing extra range.
Exit the position slowly and check that the athlete feels calmer or less guarded.
coach_cues: Exhale long, let the ribs drop, and leave the hips calmer.
common_faults: Chasing range or speed before control
Support foot collapsing or twisting
Leaning through the low back
Letting the kick or movement finish in a poor stance
breathing_cues: Exhale to organize the ribs before effort.
Do not hold breath unless specifically bracing for a loaded strength rep.
Recover nasal or calm breathing between quality attempts.
coachingExecution.quality_gate: A rep counts only if 90/90 Breathing with Hip Reset keeps pain-free range, organized breathing, clean support-foot pressure, controlled hip/trunk position, and a safe reset that supports post-kicking recovery, low-readiness reset.

dosage.notes: Use the low end when the athlete is learning, returning from layoff, in-season, sore, or carrying a high kicking/striking/soccer load. — 5-8 slow breaths

scaling.youth_beginner: Use no load, smaller range, fewer choices, and a wall or coach support when needed. Keep 90/90 Breathing with Hip Reset controlled and prioritize stance, hip, trunk, and foot position before speed or load. 1-2 light sets; stop before fatigue or frustration.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use the base version with low impact and clear success criteria; add only one variable at a time. 2 sets of clean reps or short attempts with full coaching reset.
scaling.teen: Progress range, speed, load, or partner/target complexity only after the athlete can own the support leg and recoil/finish. Use the default dose; cap volume during growth spurts or heavy sport weeks.
scaling.adult_beginner: Use regressions for balance, mobility, and impact. Prioritize pain-free positions and slower execution. Start at the low end of sets, reps, and RPE.
scaling.adult_advanced: Progress by adding intent, load, speed, smaller reaction windows, or sport-specific angles while preserving the quality gate. Use default-to-high end volume only when quality remains stable.
scaling.older_adult: Use supported versions, lower range, lower impact, and longer rests. Avoid sudden pivots or high-speed kicks unless already prepared. 1-2 sets; RPE should stay conversational except for medically cleared strength work.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use qualified guidance. Avoid breath-holding, pain, pelvic-floor symptoms, hard impact, collision, and aggressive end range; choose supported low-intensity alternatives. Short, symptom-free sets only.

avoid_after: fatiguing kick intervals before skill learning
max impact on sore groin or hamstrings
chaotic partner contact without clear roles
pairingLogic.coach_note: For kickboxing/MMA/soccer sessions, place mobility and skill mapping before output, strength after speed/skill, resilience after primary work, conditioning late, and restore last.

readiness_checks: Pain-free hip, knee, ankle, and low-back range for the chosen version
Athlete can balance or use support without compensating
Clear space, target, partner roles, and stop command when applicable
contraindications: Acute groin, hamstring, hip, knee, ankle, or low-back pain without clearance
Uncontrolled dizziness, concussion symptoms, or inability to balance safely
Contact, pad, or partner work without communication and appropriate protective equipment
common_risks: Groin or hip-flexor irritation from forcing height
Knee torque from poor support-foot pivot
Hamstring or adductor overload from too much speed or range
Fatigue-driven loss of recoil and stance

regimen.typical_frequency: 1-3x/week depending on sport practice, soreness, and phase of season; low-intensity access drills can be used more often.
regimen.in_season_note: Reduce volume and keep only the highest-transfer options when sport practices already include high kicking, sprinting, or ball-striking load.
regimen.off_season_note: Progress range, load, intent, and complexity gradually while separating high-output days from heavy tissue-capacity days when needed.

mediaInternalNotes: Film the support foot and pelvis as much as the kicking leg.
Quality beats height, load, and volume.
